Aquila: Aquila the Eagle is anchored by Altair (mag 0.8), the southernmost star of the Summer Triangle — it is easy to spot because two dimmer stars (Tarazed and Alshain) flank it closely, giving the impression of a bird's body. From Vega in Lyra, drop south across the Milky Way to find Altair, which sits almost exactly on the celestial equator. Aquila is a summer constellation, highest in the south for northern observers in August; it is visible from both hemispheres. The wings of the eagle stretch east and west of Altair along a line of fainter stars roughly aligned with the Milky Way band.
Canis Major: Canis Major contains Sirius (mag −1.46), the brightest star in the entire night sky — a blue-white beacon impossible to miss. To find it, follow the three belt stars of Orion southeast for about 20°. The constellation forms a rough stick-figure dog, with Sirius at the dog's collar and a triangle of stars (Mirzam, Adhara, Wezen) forming the hindquarters. It is best seen in winter from January through March and sits low in the south for northern hemisphere observers, but is well-placed for southern hemisphere viewers.
Cassiopeia: Cassiopeia is a bright, unmistakable W (or M) shape of five second-magnitude stars high in the northern sky. It lies on the opposite side of the celestial pole from the Big Dipper, so when the Dipper is low, Cassiopeia is high. From Polaris, simply extend the line from the end of the Dipper's handle through the pole by about the same distance to land in Cassiopeia. It is circumpolar for most northern hemisphere observers and visible year-round, crossing overhead in autumn evenings. It also sits in a rich part of the Milky Way, making it a rewarding binocular region.
Crux: Crux, the Southern Cross, is a compact kite of four bright stars with Acrux (mag 0.8) at the bottom; despite being the smallest constellation, it is the most iconic in the southern sky. It is never visible above about 25°N and is best seen from southern latitudes in autumn (April–May in the southern hemisphere). To find south, extend the long axis of the Cross (from Gacrux at top to Acrux at bottom) about 4.5 times beyond Acrux — that point is near the south celestial pole. Do not confuse it with the 'False Cross' formed by stars in Vela and Carina, which is larger and less symmetrical.
Cygnus: Cygnus the Swan is dominated by the Northern Cross asterism, with Deneb (mag 1.3) at the top and Albireo (a stunning double in binoculars) at the foot. Deneb is the northernmost star of the Summer Triangle and marks the tail of the swan; the long axis of the cross forms the body of the bird flying south along the Milky Way. From Vega in Lyra, move about 24° east-northeast to reach Deneb. Cygnus is a summer and autumn constellation, passing nearly overhead at mid-northern latitudes in August and September, and the Milky Way runs right through it.
Gemini: Gemini is identified by its twin bright stars Castor (mag 1.6) and Pollux (mag 1.1) sitting side by side roughly 4.5° apart. Each star heads a chain of fainter stars stretching south-southwest, representing the twins' bodies. From Orion's belt, move northeast past Betelgeuse and continue to find Castor and Pollux. Gemini is a winter constellation, best placed in the south for northern observers in January and February. Pollux (the slightly brighter one) is the eastern twin; a common mistake is to mix up which is which — remember 'Pollux comes after Castor' alphabetically just as east comes after west.
Leo: Leo is one of the most recognizable zodiacal constellations, shaped like a crouching lion with the Sickle asterism — a backward question mark — forming the lion's head and mane, topped by Regulus (mag 1.4) at the bottom of the hook. Regulus sits almost exactly on the ecliptic and is easy to find by extending a line from the Pointers (Merak and Dubhe in the Big Dipper) south rather than north. Leo is a spring constellation for northern observers, best placed in the south in March and April. Denebola marks the lion's tail to the east, and the right-angled triangle it forms with Regulus and Algieba is distinctive.
Orion: Orion is arguably the most recognizable constellation in the sky, featuring the perfectly straight row of three second-magnitude belt stars (Alnitak, Alnilam, Mintaka) flanked by red Betelgeuse (mag 0.4, upper left) and blue-white Rigel (mag 0.1, lower right). No star-hopping is needed — just look for this pattern in the southeast sky on winter evenings. Below the belt hangs the Orion Nebula (M42), faintly visible to the naked eye. Orion straddles the celestial equator and is visible from every inhabited location on Earth, best seen January through March.
Scorpius: Scorpius is a magnificent curved constellation shaped unmistakably like a scorpion, with red Antares (mag 1.0) marking the heart at the center and a sweeping tail curling south through the Milky Way. From Spica in Virgo, move east and slightly south about 45° to find Antares. For northern observers Scorpius hugs the southern horizon in summer (July), while southern hemisphere observers see it high overhead — the full tail is only visible from latitudes below about 35°N. Do not confuse it with nearby Ophiuchus, whose feet intrude between Scorpius and Sagittarius.
Taurus: Taurus the Bull is found by following Orion's belt stars northwest for about 20° to reach Aldebaran (mag 0.9), a distinctly orange-red star that forms the bull's eye. The V-shaped Hyades cluster forms the bull's face, and Aldebaran sits at the tip of the V (though it is not physically part of the cluster). The Pleiades star cluster (Seven Sisters) marks the bull's shoulder about 14° northwest of Aldebaran. Taurus is a winter constellation, best seen from November through February, and is prominent in the evening sky from both hemispheres.
Ursa Major: Ursa Major is best known for the Big Dipper — seven bright stars forming a ladle shape (Dubhe and Merak at the bowl's far edge, then the bent handle). The two Pointer stars (Dubhe and Merak) on the leading edge of the bowl point northward to Polaris, about 29° away. The Dipper is circumpolar for observers north of about 40°N but dips below the horizon in spring evenings for southern US observers. The full bear constellation extends well beyond the Dipper, with faint stars marking the head and feet. In spring the Dipper rides high; in autumn it is low in the north.
Ursa Minor: Ursa Minor contains Polaris (the North Star, mag 2.0) at the tip of its handle — the one nearly stationary point in the northern sky around which all other stars appear to rotate. Find it using the Big Dipper's Pointer stars (Merak and Dubhe): extend that line north about 5× the distance between them. The Little Dipper is much fainter than the Big Dipper; only Polaris and the two bowl-end stars (Kochab and Pherkad) are readily visible without dark skies. It is circumpolar for all northern hemisphere observers, but the faint stars are often washed out near cities.
Andromeda: Andromeda is best known as the home of the Andromeda Galaxy (M31), the farthest object visible to the naked eye at 2.5 million light-years. The constellation forms a curved arc of stars attached to the northeast corner of the Great Square of Pegasus, with Alpheratz (shared with Pegasus) at one end. To find M31, locate Alpheratz, then hop two stars north along a short chain and look with averted vision for a fuzzy oval. Andromeda is an autumn constellation for northern observers, best seen in October–November when it passes high overhead. It is a northern constellation and largely inaccessible from southern latitudes below ~40°S.
Aquarius: Aquarius is a faint zodiacal constellation with no first-magnitude stars, but the Y-shaped Water Jar asterism (a small tight group around Sadalsud, mag 2.9) in the upper portion is a useful anchor. Find it by starting at Fomalhaut in Piscis Austrinus — the bright solitary star in the southern autumn sky — and moving north about 45°. Aquarius lies along the ecliptic between Capricornus and Pisces and is best seen in September–October for northern observers, though it is always relatively low in the south. The Saturn Nebula (NGC 7009) and Helix Nebula are telescopic showpieces within it.
Aries: Aries is a small zodiacal constellation marked by Hamal (mag 2.0) and Sheratan (mag 2.6) — two stars about 4° apart that form a slightly curved pair pointing west toward the Pleiades. To find Aries, locate the Pleiades in Taurus and move west about 20°. The constellation has few other notable stars and is otherwise sparse. Aries is best seen in November and December for northern observers, positioned in the south during evening hours. It is historically important as the location of the vernal equinox (though precession has shifted that point into Pisces).
Auriga: Auriga is a large pentagon of stars with brilliant Capella (mag 0.1), a golden-yellow star, marking the northern vertex. Three faint but notable stars called the Kids (Epsilon, Zeta, and Eta Aurigae) sit just south of Capella. From Orion's belt, move north and slightly east, past Taurus, about 45° to reach Capella. Capella is nearly overhead at winter midnight for mid-northern latitudes (January–February) and is circumpolar north of about 44°N. It is the sixth-brightest star in the sky and hard to confuse with anything nearby.
Boötes: Boötes is anchored by Arcturus (mag −0.05), the brightest star in the northern sky and fourth-brightest overall, recognizable by its warm orange color. The surest way to find it is to follow the arc of the Big Dipper's handle ('Arc to Arcturus') southward for about 30°. The constellation forms a kite or ice-cream-cone shape with Arcturus at the bottom. Boötes is a spring and summer constellation, best seen from April to June in the northern hemisphere. The faint stars outlining the shepherd's body are sometimes hard to trace, but Arcturus itself is unmistakable.
Cancer: Cancer is the faintest of the zodiacal constellations and has no stars brighter than magnitude 3.5, making it hard to see from light-polluted skies. The key feature is the Beehive Cluster (M44, Praesepe), a naked-eye fuzzy patch in the center of the constellation that resolves into dozens of stars in binoculars. To find it, draw a line from Pollux in Gemini to Regulus in Leo — Cancer is midway along that line. It is best placed in the south for northern observers in February and March. Under dark skies look for the faint inverted-Y shape of stars around the Beehive.
Canis Minor: Canis Minor is a tiny constellation with just two easily visible stars: Procyon (mag 0.4), a brilliant white star, and Gomeisa (mag 2.9). Procyon is the eighth-brightest star in the sky and forms the Winter Triangle with Sirius and Betelgeuse. From Orion's Betelgeuse, move east-northeast about 25° to reach Procyon. The constellation is best seen in January and February for northern observers and is visible from both hemispheres. There is little else to see beyond these two stars — the 'lesser dog' body is largely imaginary.
Capricornus: Capricornus is a faint zodiacal constellation shaped like an arrowhead or bikini top pointing east, with its brightest stars around magnitude 2.8–3.1. Algedi (α Cap) and Dabih (β Cap) are close-together naked-eye doubles at the western point of the arrowhead. Find Capricornus by starting at Altair in Aquila and moving south about 17°. It sits on the ecliptic between Sagittarius and Aquarius, best seen in September–October from northern latitudes when it is due south in the evening. It is always relatively low in the south for northern observers.
Centaurus: Centaurus is a large, bright constellation of the southern sky containing the two brightest stars other than the Sun in our stellar neighborhood: Alpha Centauri (Rigil Kentaurus, mag −0.3) and Beta Centauri (Hadar, mag 0.6). These two bright stars, called the Southern Pointers, point directly to Crux (the Southern Cross) about 14° to their southwest. Centaurus is best seen from southern hemisphere latitudes in April–May, when it culminates high overhead. Northern observers above about 25°N can only see its northern edge. The constellation is also home to Omega Centauri (NGC 5139), the largest and most massive globular cluster visible to the naked eye.
Cepheus: Cepheus is a faint but recognizable constellation shaped like a child's drawing of a house — a square with a triangular roof pointing north toward Polaris. It lies between Cassiopeia and Polaris, adjoining the W of Cassiopeia on its north-northeast side. The brightest star, Alderamin (mag 2.5), marks one corner of the square. Cepheus is circumpolar for observers north of about 35°N and is best positioned in autumn evenings when it rides high overhead. It contains Mu Cephei (the Garnet Star), one of the reddest naked-eye stars, and Delta Cephei, the prototype Cepheid variable.
Corona Borealis: Corona Borealis is a small but charming semicircle of seven stars (magnitudes 2–4) shaped like a laurel wreath or crown, just east of Boötes. Gemma (Alphecca, mag 2.2) is the brightest stone in the crown. To find it, locate Arcturus in Boötes and look about 22° to the east-northeast for the curved arc of stars. It is best seen in spring and summer (May–July) for northern observers when it rides high in the southeast. The constellation is compact and easy to identify once you know what to look for — no other similar curved arc exists in that region.
Corvus: Corvus the Crow is a small but distinctive four-star quadrilateral (like a tilted square) with stars around magnitude 2.5–2.9. It sits just south and west of Spica in Virgo — look about 15° southwest of the brilliant blue-white Spica to find the compact crow shape. Alternatively, follow the arc of the Big Dipper's handle to Arcturus, then 'speed on to Spica,' and look west of Spica for Corvus. It is best seen in spring (April–May) from northern latitudes, though its southerly declination keeps it relatively low. The four-star box shape with a fifth star (Alchiba) off one corner makes it easy to recognize.
Delphinus: Delphinus the Dolphin is a tiny but delightful constellation — a compact diamond of four stars (Job's Coffin) plus a fifth trailing star, all around magnitude 3.6–4.4, that genuinely resembles a leaping dolphin. Find it by moving about 10° east-northeast of Altair in Aquila. It sits in a relatively starless region of the sky, which actually makes the tight little diamond stand out. Delphinus is a summer constellation, best seen August–September for northern observers. Two of its stars have the unusual names Sualocin and Rotanev — the reversed spelling of the Italian astronomer Nicolaus Venator.
Draco: Draco is a long, winding constellation that coils around the north celestial pole between Ursa Major and Ursa Minor, with its four-star head (the Dragon's Head or Thuban's neighborhood) northeast of Vega. The head quadrilateral — with Eltanin (mag 2.2) as the brightest eye — can be found about 25° north-northwest of Vega. The body snakes north and west, wrapping around Ursa Minor. Draco is circumpolar for observers north of about 20°N and is best traced on summer nights. Thuban in the neck was the pole star around 2700 BC.
Eridanus: Eridanus is a long, meandering river constellation that stretches from Cursa just north of Rigel in Orion all the way south to Achernar (mag 0.5), the ninth-brightest star in the sky. The northern portion is faint and winding; start at Rigel and follow the river south and west. Achernar, the river's end, is only visible from latitudes south of about 33°N. From the southern hemisphere Achernar is a prominent beacon in the south, best seen in October–November. The constellation is so long that no single season captures all of it at once.
Hercules: Hercules is a large northern constellation identified by the Keystone — a prominent quadrilateral of four stars (Kornephoros, Zeta, Eta, and Pi Herculis, magnitudes 2.8–3.9) that marks the body of the kneeling hero. Find it by drawing a line from Vega in Lyra to Arcturus in Boötes — the Keystone is about one-third of the way from Vega. The Great Globular Cluster M13, visible to the naked eye under dark skies as a faint fuzzy star, sits on the western edge of the Keystone. Hercules is a summer constellation, best seen May–August for northern observers. It is large but lacks very bright stars, making it easy to overlook until you know the Keystone.
Hydra: Hydra is the largest constellation in area and winds across a vast swath of the spring sky, but is made of mostly faint stars except Alphard (mag 2.0), the 'Heart of the Hydra.' Find Alphard about 20° south of Procyon in Canis Minor or by extending a line from Castor through Pollux in Gemini southward. The head of Hydra is a small ring of six faint stars northwest of Alphard. The body stretches eastward for over 100° below Virgo and Corvus. It is best seen in March–April for northern observers and is always relatively low in the south at mid-northern latitudes.
Lepus: Lepus the Hare crouches directly below Orion, making it one of the easiest non-prominent constellations to find — simply look south of Rigel and Saiph (Orion's southern foot stars). The constellation is a small quadrilateral with stars around magnitude 2.6–3.2, with Arneb (mag 2.6) as the brightest. It is best seen January–February for northern observers, though its southerly declination keeps it near the southern horizon at mid-northern latitudes. A notable sight within it is Hind's Crimson Star (R Leporis), one of the reddest stars visible to the naked eye when near maximum brightness.
Libra: Libra the Scales is a moderately faint zodiacal constellation with its two brightest stars, Zubenelgenubi (mag 2.7) and Zubeneschamali (mag 2.6), forming the obvious crossbeam of the scales about 7° apart. To find Libra, start at Spica in Virgo and move east about 20°. Zubenelgenubi is a wide naked-eye double with another star about 5' away. Libra lies between Virgo to the west and Scorpius to the east along the ecliptic; its stars were once considered the claws of Scorpius by ancient astronomers. It is best seen in May–June for northern observers.
Lyra: Lyra is a small but brilliant constellation anchored by Vega (mag 0.0), the fifth-brightest star in the sky and the brightest of the Summer Triangle. The constellation forms a small parallelogram south of Vega representing the lyre strings, with the double-double star Epsilon Lyrae and the Ring Nebula (M57) nearby. Vega is unmistakably blue-white and nearly overhead at mid-northern latitudes in August. It is the first star to rise in the northeast as spring turns to summer. The Ring Nebula is a small telescopic donut easily found between Beta and Gamma Lyrae.
Ophiuchus: Ophiuchus is a large constellation between Scorpius and Hercules, representing a serpent-bearer, but it is often overlooked because it straddles the serpent constellation Serpens. Rasalhague (mag 2.1) at the top is the easiest starting point — find it about 23° north of Antares in Scorpius or south of the Keystone of Hercules. The constellation's outline is roughly hexagonal, and the ecliptic passes through its southern portion (making it a 'thirteenth zodiac constellation'). Best seen in summer (June–August) for northern observers, it sits relatively high in the south in July. The rich star fields near the Ophiuchus-Scorpius border are worth scanning with binoculars.
Pegasus: Pegasus is dominated by the Great Square of Pegasus — four stars (Markab, Scheat, Algenib, and Alpheratz, the last shared with Andromeda) forming a large, nearly perfect square roughly 15° on a side with few stars inside. In autumn the Square is high overhead in the south for northern observers. From the Summer Triangle's Altair, move north-northeast about 50° to reach the Square. The Great Square is a useful guide for estimating sky transparency — how many stars you can see within it tells you how good the seeing is. Pegasus is best seen in September–November for northern hemisphere observers.
Perseus: Perseus sits in the Milky Way between Cassiopeia to the north and Taurus to the south, with Mirfak (mag 1.8) as its brightest star and a loose cluster of stars (Alpha Persei Cluster) surrounding it. Algol (the Demon Star, mag 2.1 normally), an eclipsing binary that dims to magnitude 3.4 every 2.9 days, is another famous star in Perseus. Star-hop from Cassiopeia's distinctive W southward about 25° to reach Mirfak. Perseus is best seen in autumn and winter (November–January) for northern observers and is circumpolar north of about 40°N. The Double Cluster (NGC 869/884) between Perseus and Cassiopeia is a superb naked-eye or binocular object.
Pisces: Pisces is a faint zodiacal constellation with no star brighter than magnitude 3.7, consisting of two curved chains of stars representing two fish tied together by their tails at a star called the Knot (Al Rischa, mag 3.8). The Circlet of Pisces — a small ring of five faint stars — is the head of the western fish and the most identifiable sub-pattern. Find Pisces south of the Great Square of Pegasus; the Circlet lies about 15° south-southwest of Markab. Pisces is best seen in October–November for northern observers. It currently hosts the vernal equinox, though precession has moved the actual spring equinox point here from Aries.
Piscis Austrinus: Piscis Austrinus the Southern Fish has one claim to fame: Fomalhaut (mag 1.2), one of the brightest stars in the sky and often called the 'Lonely Star of Autumn' because no other bright star appears near it in that part of the sky. Find it about 45° directly south of the Great Square of Pegasus in the September–October evening sky. From mid-northern latitudes Fomalhaut sits low in the southern sky; from the southern hemisphere it is well-placed and high. The rest of the constellation is faint and semicircular around Fomalhaut. Fomalhaut hosts a famous debris disk and a directly imaged candidate planet.
Puppis: Puppis (the Stern of the ship) is the largest piece of the ancient constellation Argo Navis, which was broken into Puppis, Vela, and Carina. Its brightest star is Naos (Zeta Puppis, mag 2.2), one of the hottest and most luminous naked-eye stars. Find Puppis by looking east of Canis Major (east of Sirius) in the winter Milky Way. It is best seen from January–March and is much better placed from the southern hemisphere; from northern latitudes above 30°N only the northern part is visible. Binoculars reveal many rich star clusters within it, including M46 and M47.
Sagittarius: Look toward the dense, bright core of the Milky Way in summer. The key asterism is the Teapot — a pattern of eight stars whose spout points left (east) toward Scorpius and whose handle sits on the right (west). The 'steam' rising from the spout is the richest part of the Milky Way. Kaus Australis (ε Sgr, mag 1.8) is the brightest star at the bottom of the spout, and Nunki (σ Sgr, mag 2.0) marks the top of the handle. From mid-northern latitudes Sagittarius hugs the southern horizon in July–August; it is best seen from the tropics or southern hemisphere.
Serpens: Serpens is unique in being the only constellation split into two disconnected parts: Serpens Caput (the head, west) and Serpens Cauda (the tail, east), divided by Ophiuchus. The head is the more identifiable section, with a small bent line of stars including Unukalhai (Alpha Serpentis, mag 2.6). Find Serpens Caput about 20° west of Rasalhague in Ophiuchus, or below Corona Borealis. Serpens Cauda lies east of Ophiuchus in the Milky Way and contains the Eagle Nebula (M16). It is best seen in summer (June–August) for northern observers.
Vela: Vela (the Sail) is another piece of the ancient ship Argo, lying east of Puppis in the southern Milky Way. Its second-magnitude stars Gamma Velorum and Delta Velorum (both around mag 1.8–2.1) are among the brightest in the constellation. The False Cross — formed by Gamma and Delta Velorum with Epsilon and Iota Carinae — can be mistaken for the Southern Cross; the False Cross is larger and less bright overall. Vela is best seen from February–April from the southern hemisphere or from low northern latitudes. Gamma Velorum (Regor) is the brightest Wolf-Rayet star visible to the naked eye.
Virgo: Virgo is the largest zodiacal constellation and second-largest overall, centered on Spica (mag 1.0), a brilliant blue-white star. To find Spica, follow the mnemonic 'Arc to Arcturus, speed on to Spica' — trace the arc of the Big Dipper's handle to orange Arcturus, then continue in the same curve south for another 30° to blue-white Spica. The constellation outlines a reclining figure with arms extended, but only Spica really stands out. Virgo is best seen from March to May and hosts the Virgo Cluster of galaxies in its northern portion. The ecliptic runs through it, so planets are frequently found nearby.
Antlia: Antlia the Air Pump is one of the faintest and least conspicuous constellations, with no star brighter than magnitude 4.3. It lies between Hydra to the north and Vela to the south, roughly 15° west of Gamma Velorum. There is no useful asterism; identify the area by noting the relative void between the more prominent Hydra and Puppis–Vela region. It is best seen from February–April and is better placed from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 45°N cannot see it at all. The brightest star, Alpha Antliae (mag 4.3), is an orange giant barely visible to the naked eye from good sites.
Apus: Apus the Bird of Paradise is a small, faint southern constellation lying between Triangulum Australe to the north and Octans (the south pole constellation) to the south. Its brightest star is Alpha Apodis (mag 3.8), an orange giant. From Triangulum Australe (a bright, compact southern triangle), move south about 20° to enter Apus. It is best seen from May–July from southern hemisphere locations; it is entirely invisible from latitudes north of 25°N. There is no distinctive shape, but its proximity to the south celestial pole makes it circumpolar from most of Australia and southern South America.
Ara: Ara the Altar is a compact southern constellation with several third-magnitude stars (Beta and Alpha Arae around mag 2.8–2.9) forming a small rectangular shape. It lies just south of Scorpius's tail, making it easy to find by dropping about 15° south of the scorpion's stinger. It is best seen from June–August from southern hemisphere locations. Northern observers above 25°N will find it very low or invisible. The Milky Way runs through Ara, making it a rewarding binocular region full of star clusters and nebulae.
Caelum: Caelum the Chisel (or Engraving Tool) is one of the smallest and faintest constellations, with its brightest star (Alpha Caeli) at only magnitude 4.4. It lies between Eridanus to the north and Pictor to the south, roughly 30° east-southeast of Rigel in Orion. There is no distinctive pattern — it is a tiny pair of faint stars with nothing memorable about the shape. It is best seen from November–January from southern latitudes. Northern observers above 40°N see very little of it, and from higher latitudes it is entirely below the horizon.
Camelopardalis: Camelopardalis the Giraffe is a large but very faint northern constellation that fills the space between Polaris and the bright stars Perseus, Auriga, and Cassiopeia. Its brightest star, Beta Camelopardalis (mag 4.0), is barely noticeable. The constellation is circumpolar from most of the northern hemisphere but is hard to identify due to its faintness; the best strategy is to note the empty-looking region north of Perseus and west of Cassiopeia. It is best seen on winter evenings when Perseus and Auriga are high. Beginners often overlook it entirely because there is nothing distinctive to grab onto.
Canes Venatici: Canes Venatici the Hunting Dogs is a small northern constellation below the handle of the Big Dipper, with Cor Caroli (mag 2.9) as its brightest and most distinctive star. Find Cor Caroli about 20° south of Alkaid (the end of the Dipper's handle). The second dog star, Chara (mag 4.3), lies 3.5° south-southwest. The constellation is best seen in spring (April–May) for northern observers. It contains the Whirlpool Galaxy (M51), one of the finest face-on spiral galaxies, found about 3.5° southwest of Alkaid and visible in binoculars from dark sites.
Carina: Carina the Keel is the brightest piece of the former Argo Navis, containing Canopus (mag −0.7), the second-brightest star in the sky. Canopus is located about 35° south of Sirius and is a reliable south-pointing beacon from the southern hemisphere. The constellation lies in a magnificent stretch of the southern Milky Way and is best seen from February–April from southern latitudes. Northern observers above about 30°N cannot see Canopus. The constellation contains the Eta Carinae Nebula (NGC 3372) and the spectacular open clusters NGC 3532 and IC 2602 (the Southern Pleiades).
Cetus: Cetus the Whale (or Sea Monster) is a large equatorial constellation with two main groups of stars connected by a chain: the head (north, near Aries) and the body (south, near Aquarius). Diphda (Beta Ceti, mag 2.0) is the brightest star and marks the southern body. Find it about 35° south of Alpheratz in Andromeda. Mira (Omicron Ceti) is a famous long-period variable that ranges from naked-eye visibility (mag 2) to telescopic faintness (mag 10) over about 332 days. Cetus is best seen in October–November for northern observers, sprawling across the autumn sky.
Chamaeleon: Chamaeleon the Chameleon is a small, faint constellation near the south celestial pole, with its brightest star (Alpha Chamaeleontis) at only magnitude 4.1. It lies between Carina to the north and Octans to the south, and can be found roughly 40° from the south pole. It is circumpolar from latitudes south of about 20°S and is completely invisible from northern latitudes above 20°N. The best season is March–May from southern locations. There is no meaningful asterism — identify the area by its location south of Carina between the False Cross and the south pole.
Circinus: Circinus the Drawing Compass is a tiny, dim constellation wedged between Alpha Centauri and Triangulum Australe in the southern sky, with its brightest star (Alpha Circini, mag 3.2) just 4° from Alpha Centauri. To find it, locate Alpha Centauri (one of the Southern Pointers to the Cross) and look about 4° south-southeast. The constellation is best seen from April–June from the southern hemisphere. It is entirely invisible from latitudes north of about 20°N. The Circinus Galaxy (NGC 5128-neighbor) is an active Seyfert galaxy in the constellation, though it requires a telescope.
Columba: Columba the Dove is a small constellation south of Lepus (which is south of Orion), with Phact (Alpha Columbae, mag 2.6) as its brightest star. Find it by moving about 20° south-southwest of Rigel in Orion, or about 15° south of Arneb in Lepus. The constellation contains a small triangle or diamond of stars, with Phact and Wazn (Beta Columbae, mag 3.1) being the most prominent. It is best seen from December–February but is always fairly low in the south for northern observers above 30°N. Mu Columbae is a runaway star moving at high speed away from the Orion Nebula.
Coma Berenices: Coma Berenices (Berenice's Hair) is a large, sparse cluster of faint stars (the Coma Star Cluster, Melotte 111) that looks like a faint fuzzy glow or loose scattering of 5th-magnitude stars in dark skies. It lies between Virgo (south), Leo (west), and Boötes (east), roughly 15° north of Denebola (Leo's tail). In binoculars the cluster resolves beautifully. The constellation also hosts the North Galactic Pole and is littered with background galaxies of the Coma Galaxy Cluster. It is best seen in spring (April–May) for northern observers. The star Diadem (Alpha Com, mag 4.3) is at the cluster's southern edge.
Corona Australis: Corona Australis the Southern Crown is a small, curved arc of modest stars (magnitudes 4–5) similar to its northern counterpart, lying just south of the Teapot asterism in Sagittarius. Look about 10° directly south of the Teapot's base (Kaus Australis) to find the semicircle. The brightest star, Alphekka Meridiana (Alpha CrA, mag 4.1), is the crown's jewel. It is best seen from June–August from the southern hemisphere and appears very low or below the horizon for observers north of about 40°N. Despite its faintness, the curved arc shape is recognizable in dark skies.
Crater: Crater the Cup is a small, faint constellation — a roughly circular ring of sixth- to fourth-magnitude stars sitting on the back of Hydra, west of Corvus. Its brightest star, Alkes (Alpha Crateris, mag 4.1), is easy to miss. Find Crater by locating Corvus (the easily seen quadrilateral south of Spica) and moving about 15° to its west. The constellation is best seen from March to May for northern observers but sits low in the south at mid-northern latitudes. It is best viewed from the southern hemisphere. The shape is a subtle circular arc that requires dark skies to trace.
Dorado: Dorado the Goldfish (or Swordfish) is a southern constellation best known for containing most of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), one of the nearest galaxies to the Milky Way, visible as a large detached patch of the Milky Way to the naked eye. The LMC alone makes Dorado unmistakable from southern latitudes. Dorado also contains the Tarantula Nebula (NGC 2070), the largest known emission nebula. Find Dorado by moving northwest from Canopus in Carina — the LMC will be obvious in dark skies as a bright cloud fragment. It is best seen from November–February and is circumpolar south of about 30°S.
Equuleus: Equuleus the Little Horse (or Little Foal) is the second-smallest constellation in the sky, a tiny diamond of four faint stars (all between magnitude 3.9 and 4.7) just west of Delphinus. Find it by moving about 10° west of Delphinus or about 9° east-northeast of Altair in Aquila. The brightest star is Kitalpha (Alpha Equulei, mag 3.9). Despite being easy to locate once you know where to look, Equuleus has little to distinguish it and is often skipped by beginners. It is best seen August–September for northern observers. It is entirely unremarkable except for its tiny size.
Fornax: Fornax the Furnace is a faint southern constellation tucked inside a large bend of Eridanus, with no star brighter than magnitude 3.9. Locate it by finding the river Eridanus as it curves south of Cetus; Fornax fills the interior of a large meander about 30° south of Cetus. The brightest star, Alpha Fornacis (mag 3.9), is a naked-eye double. It is best seen from October–December. Northern observers above 50°N cannot see it. Fornax is also home to the Fornax Galaxy Cluster and famously contains coordinates used for some deep Hubble Space Telescope field observations.
Grus: Grus the Crane is a distinctive southern constellation with a kite or diamond shape of four relatively bright stars (Al Dhanab at mag 2.1, Alnair at mag 1.7) and a chain extending north representing the crane's neck. Alnair (Alpha Gruis, mag 1.7) is easily spotted as a bright solitary star in the far south in the autumn sky. From Fomalhaut in Piscis Austrinus, move about 20° south-southeast to reach Alnair. Grus is best seen from August–October from the southern hemisphere. From mid-northern latitudes only the northern tip is ever visible above the horizon.
Horologium: Horologium the Pendulum Clock is a long, thin, faint constellation stretching roughly north-south in the southern sky between Caelum and Eridanus to the west and Reticulum and Hydrus to the south. Its brightest star, Alpha Horologii (mag 3.9), is an orange giant. Find the constellation by moving east from the southern part of Eridanus, roughly 25° east-southeast of Achernar. It is best seen from October–December from the southern hemisphere. There is no memorable asterism; this faint region is mostly notable for what it lacks — bright stars.
Hydrus: Hydrus the Lesser Water Snake (not to be confused with Hydra the larger serpent) is a southern constellation forming a triangle of stars between the Large and Small Magellanic Clouds. Its brightest star, Beta Hydri (mag 2.8), is the closest bright star to the south celestial pole, about 12° from it. Find it between the SMC (in Tucana) and the LMC (in Dorado); Beta Hydri is the brightest star in that gap. It is best seen from October–December from the southern hemisphere and is entirely invisible from northern latitudes above 10°N.
Indus: Indus the Indian is a faint southern constellation between Microscopium and Tucana, with its brightest star, Alpha Indi (mag 3.1), as the primary landmark. Find Alpha Indi about 15° northwest of Alnair in Grus, or draw a rough triangle with Grus and the Small Magellanic Cloud. The constellation forms an indistinct triangle of third- to fourth-magnitude stars. It is best seen from August–October from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above about 20°N see only a small portion of the constellation, and nothing distinctive.
Lacerta: Lacerta the Lizard is a small, faint constellation sandwiched between Cygnus to the west and Andromeda to the south, filling a nondescript region of the northern autumn sky. Its brightest star, Alpha Lacertae (mag 3.8), is hard to pick out. The best approach is to trace the outline between the more prominent constellations: it lies east of Cygnus's eastern wing and north of the Great Square of Pegasus. It is best seen from August–October for northern observers. Lacerta's main claim to fame is the class of active galactic nuclei called BL Lacertae objects, named after a variable star within it.
Leo Minor: Leo Minor the Lesser Lion is a faint constellation north of Leo and south of Ursa Major, wedged in the empty space between those two prominent constellations. Its brightest star, Praecipua (46 Leo Minoris, mag 3.8), is its sole landmark; the Alpha designation refers to a fainter star due to a historical error. Find it by looking about 20° directly north of Regulus in Leo, or about 20° south of the Big Dipper's bowl. It is best seen in March–April for northern observers. There is no memorable pattern — it is simply the faint scattered region between more notable constellations.
Lupus: Lupus the Wolf is a moderately bright southern constellation adjacent to Centaurus on the east, with several second- and third-magnitude stars including Alpha Lupi (mag 2.3) and Beta Lupi (mag 2.7). Find it by moving east from Hadar (Beta Centauri) about 15°; Lupus lies between Centaurus and Scorpius along the Milky Way. It is best seen from April–June from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 35°N see only the northern fringe of the constellation. The Milky Way runs through it, making it a rewarding binocular region. A naked-eye supernova in Lupus was recorded in 1006 AD, the brightest ever observed.
Lynx: Lynx is a very faint northern constellation occupying the large gap between Auriga and Ursa Major, named by Hevelius because only someone with the eyes of a lynx could see it. Its brightest star, Alpha Lyncis (mag 3.1), is an orange giant and the sole easily visible star. Find it roughly halfway between Capella in Auriga and Regulus in Leo, in the sparse region north of Gemini and Cancer. It is best seen from January–March for northern observers. There is essentially no recognizable pattern — it is simply a long, meandering chain of faint stars in an otherwise empty-looking sky.
Mensa: Mensa the Table Mountain is the faintest of all 88 constellations, with its brightest star (Alpha Mensae) at only magnitude 5.1; it contains no stars visible to most naked-eye observers under suburban skies. It lies directly south of Dorado and shares the southern part of the Large Magellanic Cloud with that constellation. Find it by locating the LMC and noting that its southern portion extends into Mensa. It is best seen from November–January and is circumpolar south of about 40°S. The only reason to visit Mensa is the LMC overlap — the constellation itself is otherwise invisible.
Microscopium: Microscopium the Microscope is a small, faint southern constellation with its brightest star, Gamma Microscopii (mag 4.7), barely visible to the naked eye. It lies between Sagittarius to the north, Grus to the south, and Piscis Austrinus to the west. Find it roughly 20° south of Altair in Aquila or 15° north of Alnair in Grus. It is best seen August–October from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 45°N cannot see it. There is no distinctive asterism; the constellation is a faint, undistinguished scatter of faint stars best ignored by beginners.
Monoceros: Monoceros the Unicorn lies in the heart of the Winter Triangle (formed by Sirius, Procyon, and Betelgeuse), occupying the rich Milky Way region between those three bright stars. Despite sitting in a spectacular region, the unicorn's own stars are faint (brightest at magnitude 3.9). The Rosette Nebula (NGC 2244) and open cluster NGC 2244 lie within it, as does the spectacular Christmas Tree Cluster (NGC 2264). Find the constellation by locating the midpoint of the triangle formed by Sirius, Procyon, and Betelgeuse. It is best seen January–February for northern observers.
Musca: Musca the Fly is a small, compact southern constellation adjacent to Crux (the Southern Cross), lying just south of it and east of Carina. Its four main stars (Alpha, Beta, Delta, Lambda Muscae) form a small diamond, with Alpha Muscae (mag 2.7) as the brightest. To find Musca, simply look about 5° south of the southern end of Crux (below Acrux). It is best seen from March–May from the southern hemisphere and is entirely invisible from latitudes north of about 20°N. The compact, symmetric diamond shape is easy to recognize once you know it is south of the Southern Cross.
Norma: Norma the Carpenter's Square is a small, faint constellation embedded in the southern Milky Way between Scorpius and Centaurus, with no star brighter than magnitude 4.0 (its original brightest stars were reassigned to Scorpius). Find it about 15° east of Zeta Centauri or just northwest of the tail of Scorpius. Despite its faintness, the region is rich in star clusters and Milky Way texture. It is best seen May–July from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 30°N cannot see it. Gamma-2 Normae (mag 4.0) is the brightest remaining star after the boundary revisions.
Octans: Octans the Octant contains the south celestial pole but is extremely faint — the south pole star, Sigma Octantis, is only magnitude 5.4 and barely visible to the naked eye even from ideal sites. Unlike the north pole (marked by bright Polaris), the southern pole has no bright guiding star. The practical way to find the south celestial pole is to extend the long axis of Crux beyond Acrux by about 4.5 cross-lengths, or to bisect the line between the Southern Pointers (Alpha and Beta Centauri) perpendicular to them. Octans is circumpolar south of the equator. The lack of a true south pole star is frequently confusing for southern observers.
Pavo: Pavo the Peacock is a southern constellation with Peacock (Alpha Pavonis, mag 1.9) as its bright anchor star, one of the more conspicuous southern stars. Find Peacock about 20° south-southeast of Alnair in Grus, or roughly halfway between the direction of Sagittarius and the south celestial pole. The constellation forms a modest fan or kite shape south of Telescopium. It is best seen from July–September from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 30°N cannot see Peacock at all. The kite shape formed by Alpha through Delta Pavonis is moderately easy to trace.
Phoenix: Phoenix the Phoenix is a southern constellation with Ankaa (Alpha Phoenicis, mag 2.4) as its brightest and most conspicuous star. Find Ankaa about 30° south of Fomalhaut (the Lonely Star of Autumn) or about 40° west of Achernar in Eridanus. The constellation forms a rough Y or bird shape, with Ankaa at the southern base. It is best seen from October–December from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 32°N see only the very top of the constellation. Despite being in a sparse area of sky, Ankaa is bright enough to serve as a useful southern hemisphere guide star.
Pictor: Pictor the Painter's Easel is a small, faint southern constellation between Canopus in Carina and Achernar in Eridanus, with its brightest star, Alpha Pictoris (mag 3.2), barely conspicuous. Find it roughly 30° east of Canopus along the southern Milky Way edge. The star Beta Pictoris (mag 3.9) is famous for its edge-on debris disk and directly imaged planets — it is one of the best-studied planetary system formation sites. Pictor is best seen December–February from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 26°N cannot see it.
Pyxis: Pyxis the Compass (mariner's compass, not drawing compass) is a small, faint constellation just north of Vela and east of Puppis in the ship Argo region. Its brightest star, Alpha Pyxidis (mag 3.7), sits in the southern Milky Way. Find Pyxis about 15° north of Gamma Velorum or roughly halfway between Sirius and Gamma Velorum. It is best seen from January–March, though it remains low in the south for northern observers above 35°N. The constellation was carved out of the mast of Argo Navis by Lacaille and is sometimes considered the ship's compass.
Reticulum: Reticulum the Reticle (cross-hairs) is a small southern constellation near the Large Magellanic Cloud and between Horologium and Dorado, with its brightest star, Alpha Reticuli (mag 3.4), being an easy naked-eye point in the southern sky. To find it, locate the LMC and then look about 20° southeast of it — Reticulum is the small diamond-shaped pattern of four stars in that region. It is best seen from October–January from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 23°N cannot see it. The reticle shape — a small rhombus — is one of the more recognizable of the southern minor constellations.
Sagitta: Sagitta the Arrow is a tiny but unmistakable constellation in the summer Milky Way — it genuinely resembles an arrow pointing east, with a shaft and a four-star head (the arrowhead). At only 80 square degrees it is the third-smallest constellation. Find it about 10° south of Albireo (the head of Cygnus) or midway between Altair (Aquila) and Albireo. Its four main stars range from magnitude 3.5 to 4.4. It is best seen August–September for northern observers. The globular cluster M71 is an easy binocular object within Sagitta, sitting along the arrow's shaft.
Sculptor: Sculptor the Sculptor's Workshop (or Studio) is a large but very faint southern constellation south of Aquarius and Piscis Austrinus, with no star brighter than magnitude 4.3. The brightest star, Alpha Sculptoris (mag 4.3), is barely conspicuous. Find the constellation area about 25° south of Fomalhaut. Despite its faintness, Sculptor is interesting because it contains the south galactic pole and the Sculptor Galaxy (NGC 253), one of the nearest large spiral galaxies and a spectacular sight in binoculars or a small telescope. It is best seen October–November from both hemispheres.
Scutum: Scutum the Shield is a small constellation in the Milky Way just south of Aquila, containing one of the richest parts of the galactic plane. Its brightest star, Alpha Scuti (mag 3.8), is hard to notice, but the Scutum Star Cloud — a brilliant mass of unresolved stars in the Milky Way — makes the region obvious to the naked eye as an especially bright patch of the summer Milky Way. Find Scutum about 10° south of Altair in Aquila. The Wild Duck Cluster (M11) is a spectacular open cluster in Scutum, one of the richest known. It is best seen July–August from both hemispheres.
Sextans: Sextans the Sextant is a faint equatorial constellation south of Leo and Regulus, with no star brighter than magnitude 4.5. It fills the region roughly 15–20° south of Regulus between Leo and Hydra. Find it by moving about 15° due south of Regulus and looking for a sparse scatter of faint stars. The only notable object is the Sextans Dwarf, a faint dwarf spheroidal galaxy of the Local Group. Sextans is best seen from March–April for northern observers. It is a modern constellation (named by Hevelius in 1687) with no mythology and no memorable pattern.
Telescopium: Telescopium the Telescope is a small, faint southern constellation between Corona Australis to the north and Pavo to the south, with its brightest star, Alpha Telescopii (mag 3.5), as the sole reasonable landmark. Find it about 20° south of Corona Australis (which sits below Sagittarius), or about 15° north of Peacock (Alpha Pavonis). The constellation forms a thin triangular arc of faint stars. It is best seen July–August from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 33°N cannot see it. There is no memorable asterism.
Triangulum: Triangulum the Triangle is a small northern constellation between Andromeda and Aries, forming an obvious narrow elongated triangle of three stars (Mothallah at mag 3.4, Beta at mag 3.0, Gamma at mag 4.0). Find it about 20° south of Andromeda's main chain or 12° northwest of the Pleiades. It is best seen from October–December for northern observers. Triangulum's main attraction is M33 (the Triangulum Galaxy), the third-largest member of the Local Group, which is visible as a large faint glow under excellent dark skies using averted vision. M33 is notoriously difficult to see due to its low surface brightness despite being large.
Triangulum Australe: Triangulum Australe the Southern Triangle is a compact, bright equilateral triangle of stars in the southern Milky Way between Centaurus and the Norma region, with Atria (Alpha Trianguli Australis, mag 1.9) as the brightest and southernmost vertex. It is one of the most easily recognized of the far-southern constellations because of its compact, symmetrical shape and the brightness of its three main stars. Find it about 25° east of the Southern Pointers (Alpha and Beta Centauri). It is best seen from April–June from the southern hemisphere and is entirely invisible north of about 25°N.
Tucana: Tucana the Toucan is a southern constellation best known for hosting the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) — a detached patch of faint milky glow visible to the naked eye from dark southern sites — and the magnificent globular cluster 47 Tucanae (NGC 104) which appears to the naked eye as a fuzzy star right next to the SMC. Find Tucana by locating the SMC (a detached piece of the Milky Way visible in the far south) about 20° east of Achernar in Eridanus. The brightest stellar anchor is Alpha Tucanae (mag 2.9). It is best seen from September–November from southern latitudes.
Volans: Volans the Flying Fish is a small, faint southern constellation between Carina and the Large Magellanic Cloud, with its brightest stars (Gamma Volantis, mag 3.8) barely conspicuous. Find it about 25° north-northeast of Canopus, or wedged between the LMC to the south and the bright star fields of Carina to the north. It is best seen from January–March from the southern hemisphere. Northern observers above 14°N cannot see it. There is no striking pattern — Volans is most useful as a way of noting that you are in the vicinity of Carina and Pictor when star-hopping in the deep south.
Vulpecula: Vulpecula the Fox is a small, faint constellation in the summer Milky Way between Cygnus to the north and Aquila to the south, straddling the shaft of Sagitta the Arrow. Its brightest star, Anser (Alpha Vulpeculae, mag 4.4), is barely conspicuous. The easiest approach is to find Sagitta (the tiny arrow south of Albireo) and look just north of it. Vulpecula's most famous object is the Dumbbell Nebula (M27), the largest and brightest planetary nebula in the sky and an easy binocular target under dark skies. It is best seen from July–September for northern observers.
